title: Combinations
code: public IList<IList<int>> Combine(int n, int k)
for (int i = start; i <= n - (k - path.Count) + 1; i++)

frames.caption: Choose k=2 of the numbers 1..4. The start index makes the selection unordered, so each of the C(4,2) combinations is generated exactly once.
frames.note: A combination is an UNORDERED selection — {1,2} and {2,1} are the same. We walk a decision tree of depth k: at each level pick the next (larger) number. Because we only ever move forward from a start index, we never produce both {a,b} and {b,a}. The number of leaves is C(n,k); for n=4, k=2 that is 6.

frames.caption: Need 2 more; loop i from 1 to 3 (prune stops at n-(k-path.Count)+1).
frames.note: The loop only ever starts at `start` and moves FORWARD, never revisiting earlier numbers. That single rule makes selection unordered: {1,2} is generated but {2,1} never is, so each combination appears exactly once and no used[] array is needed. The upper bound n-(k-path.Count)+1 is the prune: if fewer than the (k - path.Count) still-needed numbers remain, no completion is possible, so we stop early.

frames.caption: i=1: CHOOSE 1 -> path becomes [1]. Explore Backtrack(2).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append i to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i+1. Passing i+1 (never i) means we look strictly forward, so element i is used at most once and no permutation duplicate is produced. This is the same start-index skeleton subsets use; the only difference is the size-k base case.

frames.caption: Need 1 more; loop i from 2 to 4 (prune stops at n-(k-path.Count)+1).
frames.note: The loop only ever starts at `start` and moves FORWARD, never revisiting earlier numbers. That single rule makes selection unordered: {1,2} is generated but {2,1} never is, so each combination appears exactly once and no used[] array is needed. The upper bound n-(k-path.Count)+1 is the prune: if fewer than the (k - path.Count) still-needed numbers remain, no completion is possible, so we stop early.

frames.caption: i=2: CHOOSE 2 -> path becomes [1,2]. Explore Backtrack(3).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append i to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i+1. Passing i+1 (never i) means we look strictly forward, so element i is used at most once and no permutation duplicate is produced. This is the same start-index skeleton subsets use; the only difference is the size-k base case.

frames.caption: path.Count == k (2). Record combination [1,2] (combo #1).
frames.note: BASE CASE: when the path has reached length k we have a complete combination, so we record a COPY and return. We record only at depth k (unlike subsets, which record at every node) because a combination is a fixed-size selection. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list.

frames.caption: Recorded [1,2] -> 1 combination so far. Return to the caller.
frames.note: BASE CASE: when the path has reached length k we have a complete combination, so we record a COPY and return. We record only at depth k (unlike subsets, which record at every node) because a combination is a fixed-size selection. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list.

frames.caption: Back at start=2, i=2: UN-CHOOSE 2 -> path is [1] again. Continue to the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the number back off the path so the next sibling branch starts from a clean slate. This rewind is what makes it "backtracking" — without it, siblings would inherit each other's choices. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: Done: 6 combinations = C(4,2). The recursion tree had depth k=2 and explored only forward picks, so each combination appeared once.
frames.note: There are exactly C(n,k) combinations. The start index keeps selection forward-only (unordered), and the n-(k-path.Count)+1 bound prunes branches that can no longer reach size k. Time is O(k * C(n,k)) — C(n,k) combinations, each copied in O(k); space (excluding output) is O(k) for the recursion stack.
